adverse possession.title: Adverse Possession (India)
adverse possession.summary: Adverse possession is a legal doctrine under Indian law that allows a person to claim ownership of land if they have possessed it openly, continuously, and hostilely for a statutory period. The principle is based on the maxim 'Vigilantibus non dormientibus jura subveniunt' — the law assists those who are vigilant, not those who sleep over their rights.
points: Continuous and uninterrupted possession for 12 years (private property) or 30 years (government property)
Possession must be hostile — without the owner's permission or consent
Possession must be open, notorious, and visible to the world
The possessor must have the intention to possess the property as their own (animus possidendi)
The burden of proof lies on the person claiming adverse possession
Once the limitation period expires, the original owner's right to sue is extinguished
case_references: State of Haryana v. Mukesh Kumar (2011) — SC clarified adverse possession as a 'law of the rich against the poor'
Hemaji Waghaji Jat v. Bhikhabhai Khengarbhai Harijan (2009) — SC recommended Parliament to reconsider the law
adverse possession.practical_notes: Courts in India have increasingly criticised adverse possession, noting it rewards a trespasser. Legislative reform has been recommended but not yet enacted.

contract law.title: Contract Law Overview (India)
contract law.summary: The Indian Contract Act, 1872 is the primary legislation governing contracts in India. It defines the essentials of a valid contract, types of contracts, performance of contracts, breach, and remedies available. All agreements enforceable by law are contracts.
points: Offer (Proposal) and Acceptance — Sections 2(a) and 2(b)
Lawful consideration — Section 2(d); every contract requires consideration except gifts under Sec 25
Capacity to contract — Section 11; parties must be of age of majority, sound mind, not disqualified by law
Free consent — Section 14; absence of coercion, undue influence, fraud, misrepresentation, or mistake
Lawful object — Section 23; the object must not be illegal, immoral, or opposed to public policy
Void agreements — Sections 24–30 (e.g., agreements in restraint of trade, marriage, or legal proceedings)
Breach of contract — Section 73 (damages), Section 74 (liquidated damages)
case_references: Mohori Bibee v. Dharmodas Ghose (1903) — Contract by a minor is void ab initio
Balfour v. Balfour — Domestic agreements lack intent to create legal relations
Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co. — General offer and acceptance principles
contract law.practical_notes: Specific Relief Act, 1963 provides for specific performance when damages are inadequate. Sale of Goods Act, 1930 and Partnership Act, 1932 also supplement contract law.

ai regulations.title: AI Regulations in India
ai regulations.summary: India does not yet have a dedicated AI-specific legislation. The regulatory approach is evolving through advisories, data protection laws, and sectoral regulations. MEITY (Ministry of Electronics and IT) has issued guidelines, and the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP), 2023 has implications for AI systems processing personal data.
points: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP), 2023 — regulates processing of digital personal data, relevant to AI/ML systems
IT Act, 2000 — covers cyber crimes, data protection, and electronic governance; Sec 43A for data breach liability
MEITY Advisories (2024) — mandated labeling of AI-generated content and prior government approval for untested AI models
NITI Aayog's Responsible AI Framework — principles-based approach covering fairness, transparency, accountability
RBI guidelines on AI in financial services — mandates explainability and fairness in credit scoring AI
No comprehensive AI Act yet — India follows a sectoral, principles-based regulatory model
ai regulations.law: DPDP Act, 2023; IT Act, 2000; MEITY Advisories
ai regulations.practical_notes: India's approach balances innovation with regulation. Startups should track MEITY advisories and ensure DPDP compliance when deploying AI systems processing Indian citizens' data.

property disputes.title: Property Disputes in India
property disputes.summary: Property disputes in India typically involve conflicts over title, possession, boundaries, or inheritance. They are governed by multiple laws depending on the nature of the dispute and are adjudicated in civil courts. Property matters constitute one of the largest categories of civil litigation in India.
points: Title suits — to establish legal ownership (governed by Transfer of Property Act, 1882)
Partition suits — division of jointly held property among co-owners
Injunction suits — to prevent illegal interference with possession or enjoyment
Specific performance — compelling a party to complete a property transaction
Encroachment disputes — unlawful occupation of another's property
Registration of property — compulsory under Registration Act, 1908 for immovable property above ₹100
property disputes.law: Transfer of Property Act, 1882; Registration Act, 1908; Indian Succession Act, 1925
case_references: Suraj Lamp & Industries Pvt. Ltd. v. State of Haryana (2012) — SC held that sale agreements and GPA sales are not valid modes of transfer
K. Kameswara Rao v. A. Kathyayani (2020) — burden of proof in title disputes
property disputes.practical_notes: Always verify title through encumbrance certificate, mutation records, and chain of ownership before purchasing property. RERA (Real Estate Regulation Act, 2016) provides additional protections for homebuyers.

fundamental rights.title: Fundamental Rights under the Indian Constitution
fundamental rights.summary: Part III of the Indian Constitution (Articles 12–35) guarantees Fundamental Rights to all citizens. These rights are justiciable and enforceable through the Supreme Court (Article 32) and High Courts (Article 226). They form the backbone of Indian constitutional democracy.
points: Right to Equality (Articles 14–18) — equality before law, prohibition of discrimination, abolition of untouchability and titles
Right to Freedom (Articles 19–22) — six freedoms including speech, assembly, movement; protection of life and personal liberty
Right against Exploitation (Articles 23–24) — prohibition of trafficking, forced labour, and child labour
Right to Freedom of Religion (Articles 25–28) — freedom of conscience, practice, propagation, and management of religious affairs
Cultural and Educational Rights (Articles 29–30) — protection of minorities' interests, right to establish educational institutions
Right to Constitutional Remedies (Article 32) — 'heart and soul of the Constitution' per Dr. Ambedkar
Right to Privacy — recognized as a Fundamental Right under Article 21 by the SC in 2017
case_references: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) — Basic Structure Doctrine
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) — expanded scope of Article 21
K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) — Right to Privacy as Fundamental Right
fundamental rights.practical_notes: Fundamental Rights are not absolute — they are subject to reasonable restrictions. Article 32 allows direct approach to the Supreme Court via writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo Warranto).

ipc offences.title: Indian Penal Code — Key Offences
ipc offences.summary: The Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860 was the primary criminal code of India (now being replaced by the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023). It classified criminal offences, defined punishments, and established the framework for criminal liability in India.
points: Section 302 — Murder (punishment: death or life imprisonment)
Section 304 — Culpable homicide not amounting to murder
Section 376 — Rape (stringent punishment post-2013 amendment)
Section 420 — Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property
Section 498A — Cruelty by husband or relatives (dowry-related harassment)
Section 354 — Assault or criminal force to outrage modesty of a woman
Section 307 — Attempt to murder
Section 120B — Criminal conspiracy
ipc offences.law: Indian Penal Code, 1860 (being replaced by Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 — BNS)
case_references: Bachan Singh v. State of Punjab (1980) — 'rarest of rare' doctrine for death penalty
Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan (1997) — guidelines on sexual harassment at workplace
ipc offences.practical_notes: The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 replaces the IPC effective from July 1, 2024. Key provisions are retained with new section numbers and some additions like community service as punishment.

bail.title: Bail Provisions in India
bail.summary: Bail is the temporary release of an accused person awaiting trial, upon furnishing a bond or surety. Indian law distinguishes between bailable and non-bailable offences. The principle is 'bail is the rule, jail is the exception.'
points: Bailable offences (Section 436 CrPC) — bail is a matter of right; police must grant it
Non-bailable offences (Section 437 CrPC) — bail is at the court's discretion
Anticipatory bail (Section 438 CrPC) — pre-arrest bail when apprehending arrest
Default bail (Section 167(2) CrPC) — if chargesheet not filed within 60/90 days
Bail conditions — may include surrender of passport, regular attendance, travel restrictions
Cancellation of bail (Section 439(2) CrPC) — if conditions violated or accused absconds
bail.law: Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC), 1973 — Sections 436–450 (now BNSS, 2023)
case_references: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar (2014) — SC guidelines on arrest in Sec 498A cases
Satender Kumar Antil v. CBI (2022) — SC mandated bail for offences up to 7 years
bail.practical_notes: When applying for bail, courts consider: nature and gravity of offence, criminal antecedents, likelihood of fleeing justice, and possibility of tampering with evidence.

consumer protection.title: Consumer Protection in India
consumer protection.summary: The Consumer Protection Act, 2019 replaced the 1986 Act and provides enhanced protection to consumers. It covers goods and services including e-commerce, establishes Consumer Dispute Redressal Commissions at district, state, and national levels, and introduces product liability and unfair trade practices provisions.
points: Six consumer rights — Right to safety, information, choice, hearing, redressal, and consumer education
Three-tier redressal mechanism — District Commission (up to ₹1 crore), State Commission (₹1–10 crore), National Commission (above ₹10 crore)
E-commerce rules — apply to all online marketplaces and sellers operating in India
Product liability — manufacturers, sellers, and service providers can be held liable for defective goods/services
Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) — empowered to take suo moto action
Complaint filing — can be done online via the e-Daakhil portal
Misleading advertisements — Section 2(28) defines and penalizes misleading ads
consumer protection.law: Consumer Protection Act, 2019; Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules, 2020
case_references: Indian Medical Association v. VP Shantha (1995) — medical services fall under consumer protection
Lucknow Development Authority v. M.K. Gupta (1994) — deficiency in service by government bodies
consumer protection.practical_notes: Consumers can file complaints online at edaakhil.nic.in. No lawyer is required. Complaints must be filed within 2 years from the cause of action.

divorce law.title: Divorce Law in India
divorce law.summary: Divorce in India is governed by personal laws based on religion, and the secular Special Marriage Act. Each law specifies grounds for divorce, maintenance, custody, and property division. Mutual consent divorce is the most common and fastest route.
points: Hindu Marriage Act, 1955 — Sec 13 (contested divorce), Sec 13B (mutual consent; requires 6-month cooling period)
Muslim Personal Law — Talaq (various forms), Khula (wife-initiated), Mubarat (mutual)
Special Marriage Act, 1954 — secular law applicable to inter-religious marriages; Sec 27 (divorce)
Indian Divorce Act, 1869 — applicable to Christians
Grounds for divorce — cruelty, adultery, desertion (2+ years), conversion, unsoundness of mind, communicable disease
Maintenance — Section 125 CrPC provides maintenance irrespective of religion
Child custody — governed by 'welfare of the child' principle
divorce law.law: Hindu Marriage Act, 1955; Special Marriage Act, 1954; Muslim Personal Law; Indian Divorce Act, 1869
case_references: Shayara Bano v. Union of India (2017) — SC struck down Triple Talaq as unconstitutional
Amardeep Singh v. Harveen Kaur (2017) — SC allowed waiver of 6-month cooling period in mutual consent divorce
divorce law.practical_notes: Mutual consent divorce typically takes 6–18 months. Contested divorces can take years. Mediation is encouraged and courts often refer cases to mediation centres.

right to information.title: Right to Information (RTI) in India
right to information.summary: The Right to Information Act, 2005 empowers Indian citizens to access information from public authorities. It promotes transparency and accountability in governance. Any citizen can file an RTI application to seek information from government bodies.
points: Any Indian citizen can file an RTI application — no reason needs to be given for seeking information
Public authorities must respond within 30 days (48 hours if life/liberty is at stake)
Application fee — ₹10 for central government departments
Two appeals — First Appeal to the designated officer, Second Appeal to the Information Commission
Exemptions under Section 8 — national security, cabinet papers, trade secrets, personal privacy
Central Information Commission (CIC) and State Information Commissions — appellate bodies
Penalties — up to ₹25,000 for non-compliance by Public Information Officer
case_references: CBSE v. Aditya Bandopadhyay (2011) — SC held RTI cannot be used as a tool for harassment
Supreme Court of India v. Subhash Chandra Agarwal (2019) — office of CJI is a public authority under RTI
right to information.practical_notes: RTI applications can be filed online at rtionline.gov.in for central government departments. State-level RTIs have separate portals.

gst.title: Goods and Services Tax (GST) in India
gst.summary: GST is a comprehensive indirect tax levied on the manufacture, sale, and consumption of goods and services in India. Introduced on July 1, 2017 via the 101st Constitutional Amendment, it replaced multiple cascading taxes and follows a dual structure (Central GST + State GST).
points: Dual GST structure — CGST (Central) + SGST (State) for intra-state, IGST for inter-state transactions
Four main tax slabs — 5%, 12%, 18%, and 28% (with some items at 0% or exempt)
GST registration mandatory if turnover exceeds ₹40 lakh (₹20 lakh for services / special category states)
Composition scheme — simplified compliance for small taxpayers with turnover up to ₹1.5 crore
Input Tax Credit (ITC) — taxes paid on inputs can be claimed as credit against output tax liability
GST Network (GSTN) — IT backbone managing registration, returns, and compliance
GST Council — constitutional body (Article 279A) that recommends rates and policy changes
gst.law: Central GST Act, 2017; IGST Act, 2017; State GST Acts; Constitution (101st Amendment) Act, 2016
gst.practical_notes: GST returns include GSTR-1 (outward supplies), GSTR-3B (summary return), and annual return GSTR-9. Late filing attracts penalties and interest.

cyber crime.title: Cyber Crime Laws in India
cyber crime.summary: Cyber crimes in India are primarily governed by the Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended in 2008) and relevant IPC/BNS provisions. The Act covers hacking, identity theft, cyber terrorism, data breaches, and online fraud.
points: Section 66 IT Act — computer-related offences (hacking) — up to 3 years imprisonment and ₹5 lakh fine
Section 66C — identity theft — up to 3 years and ₹1 lakh fine
Section 66D — cheating by personation using computer resources
Section 66E — violation of privacy (capturing/publishing private images)
Section 67 — publishing obscene material in electronic form
Section 43A — compensation for failure to protect data
Section 69 — government power to intercept, monitor, or decrypt information
Section 79 — safe harbour for intermediaries (subject to due diligence)
cyber crime.law: Information Technology Act, 2000; IT (Amendment) Act, 2008; IT (Intermediary Guidelines) Rules, 2021
case_references: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) — SC struck down Section 66A as unconstitutional (restriction on free speech)
K.N. Govindacharya v. Union of India (2015) — intermediary liability and due diligence
cyber crime.practical_notes: Cyber crime complaints can be filed online at cybercrime.gov.in. For financial fraud, immediately call 1930 (National Cyber Crime Helpline).

labour law.title: Labour Laws in India
labour law.summary: India has consolidated its 29 central labour laws into 4 Labour Codes: Code on Wages, Industrial Relations, Social Security, and Occupational Safety. These codes aim to simplify compliance and extend protections to informal and gig workers.
points: Code on Wages, 2019 — universal minimum wage, timely payment, equal remuneration
Industrial Relations Code, 2020 — trade unions, standing orders, strikes/lockouts, retrenchment
Social Security Code, 2020 — EPF, ESI, gratuity, maternity benefits; extends to gig/platform workers
OSH Code, 2020 — occupational safety, health, and working conditions; applies to establishments with 10+ workers
Minimum Wages — set by central and state governments; vary by region and skill level
Gratuity — payable after 5 years of continuous service (Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972)
Maternity Benefit — 26 weeks of paid leave (Maternity Benefit Act, 1961, amended 2017)
labour law.law: Code on Wages, 2019; Industrial Relations Code, 2020; Social Security Code, 2020; OSH Code, 2020
labour law.practical_notes: The four codes have been passed by Parliament but not yet fully notified in all states. Most states still operate under the older laws pending notification.

writ jurisdiction.title: Writ Jurisdiction in India
writ jurisdiction.summary: Under the Indian Constitution, the Supreme Court (Article 32) and High Courts (Article 226) can issue writs to enforce Fundamental Rights and for other purposes. This jurisdiction is a powerful constitutional remedy against arbitrary state action.
points: Habeas Corpus — 'produce the body'; used against illegal detention or imprisonment
Mandamus — commands a public authority to perform a duty it has failed to do
Prohibition — prevents a lower court or tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction
Certiorari — quashes an order of a lower court or tribunal that has acted beyond jurisdiction
Quo Warranto — challenges a person's right to hold a public office
Article 32 — available only for Fundamental Rights enforcement; directly in Supreme Court
Article 226 — broader scope than Article 32; High Courts can issue writs for any purpose
writ jurisdiction.law: Constitution of India — Article 32 (Supreme Court), Article 226 (High Courts)
case_references: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras (1950) — Article 32 is a fundamental right in itself
A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras (1950) — early interpretation of habeas corpus and Article 21
writ jurisdiction.practical_notes: Article 32 cannot be suspended even during a national emergency (44th Amendment). However, the right to move the court for enforcement of rights under Articles 20 and 21 cannot be suspended at all.

motor accident claims.title: Motor Accident Claims in India
motor accident claims.summary: Motor accident claims in India are governed by the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988. Victims of road accidents (or their legal heirs) can claim compensation from the Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal (MACT) regardless of fault under third-party insurance.
points: No-fault liability — Section 140 provides compensation regardless of fault (up to ₹50,000 for injury, ₹2 lakh for death)
Fault-based claims — Section 166; unlimited compensation based on multiplier method
Structured formula — National Insurance Co. v. Pranay Sethi (2017) SC judgment standardized computation
Hit and run cases — compensation from the Solatium Scheme Fund managed by the government
Third-party insurance — mandatory for all vehicles; covers injury/death to third parties
Limitation period — claims must be filed within 6 months (condonable up to reasonable time)
case_references: National Insurance Co. v. Pranay Sethi (2017) — SC standardized multiplier method for compensation
Sarla Verma v. Delhi Transport Corporation (2009) — earlier guidelines on multiplier method
motor accident claims.practical_notes: MACT claims do not require court fees. Victims should file an FIR, obtain medical records, and approach the nearest MACT. Legal aid is available for indigent claimants.
